README.html.title: WinInteropUtils | WinInteropUtils
README.html.summary: WinInteropUtils WinInteropUtils is a large C# P/Invoke library built on .NET 8 that provides many functions and wrappers around Windows API functions, structures, and others. You can download WinInteropUtils on NuGet or download the DLL directly from the Releases page. The base namespace is FireBlade.WinInteropUtils. For WinInteropUtils.WinForms, the namespace is FireBlade.WinInteropUtils.WinForms. Documentation For documentation, visit the official documentation page.

WinInteropUtils PE Resource Utility/Sett.title: Settings system | WinInteropUtils
WinInteropUtils PE Resource Utility/Sett.summary: Settings system The PE Resource Utility's settings system is a configuration storage system based on a combination of JSON files and UserControls. Structure Directories All configuration-related files are in the SettingsPages or Config folder in the root of the project. The Config folder contains .settings files for each page. The SettingsPages folder contains a Data subfolder containing JSON files for each in-app settings or property panel, which list all the pages contained within. The SettingsPages folder also contains subdirectories for each settings or property panel, that contain the actual pages themselves. The root of the SettingsPages folder also contains the definition of the IConfigPage interface. Pages Each page is represented by a UserControl that implements the IConfigPage interface within /SettingsPages/<DIALOG>. The page should also have an associated .settings file within /Config for storing its settings. The IConfigPage interface The IConfigPage interface is an interface that all pages should implement. This interface provides the following methods for your page to handle: void ApplyChanges() Applies the changes made in the configuration page. void CancelPreview() Fires whenever the user presses the Cancel or OK button, or navigates to a different page. This method gives the page an opportunity to undo any previews. For example, if the page shows a live preview of a setting, it can undo it in this method. bool GetSettingsModified() Fires whenever the settings dialog needs to query whether the page was modified. The interface also provides the OnModify event, which you should Invoke whenever the modification flag that is retrieved with GetSettingsModified changes. If you have a lot of settings, it may be useful to make a helper like this: private void SetModified() { _modified = true; OnModify?.Invoke(this, EventArgs.Empty); } Creating a page To create a page: Identify the JSON file of the settings dialog you want to add the page to within /SettingsPages/Data. Add the required info for the page to the JSON file. The schema may differ based on which dialog you're adding the page to, based on each page's requirements. Usually, each page should have a UniqueId property. Note it down, as it will be needed later. Create a new UserControl within /SettingsPages/<DIALOG> and name it <UNIQUE_ID>Page. Create a new .settings file for your page within /Config and add your settings. Add the controls you need on the settings page in the designer and wire up their event handlers. For most pages, these event handlers should modify Config.<SETTINGS_FILE_NAME>, but should not call Save. Implement IConfigPage. If you wired up your event handlers to modify the .settings file, you should call Config.<SETTINGS_FILE_NAME>.Default.Save inside ApplyChanges. Build and run the application. Go to the dialog where you added the page. If you did everything correctly, the page should show up and you should be able to click on it to access its contents.

api/FireBlade.WinInteropUtils.ClassStyle.title: Enum ClassStyle | WinInteropUtils
api/FireBlade.WinInteropUtils.ClassStyle.summary: Enum ClassStyle Namespace FireBlade.WinInteropUtils Assembly WinInteropUtils.dll Represents class styles. Class styles define additional elements of the window class. [Flags] public enum ClassStyle Extension Methods EnumExtensions.GetValues(Enum) Fields ByteAlignClient = 4096 Aligns the window's client area on a byte boundary (in the x direction). This style affects the width of the window and its horizontal placement on the display. ByteAlignWindow = 8192 Aligns the window on a byte boundary (in the x direction). This style affects the width of the window and its horizontal placement on the display. ClassDeviceContext = 64 Allocates one device context to be shared by all windows in the class. Because window classes are process specific, it is possible for multiple threads of an application to create a window of the same class. It is also possible for the threads to attempt to use the device context simultaneously. When this happens, the system allows only one thread to successfully finish its drawing operation. DoubleClicks = 8 Sends a double-click message to the window procedure when the user double-clicks the mouse while the cursor is within a window belonging to the class. DropShadow = 131072 Enables the drop shadow effect on a window. Typically, this is enabled for small, short-lived windows such as menus to emphasize their Z-order relationship to other windows. Windows created from a class with this style must be top-level windows; they may not be child windows. GlobalClass = 16384 Indicates that the window class is an application global class. For more information, see Application Global Classes. HRedraw = 2 Redraws the entire window if a movement or size adjustment changes the width of the client area. NoClose = 512 Disables Close on the window menu. OwnDeviceContext = 32 Allocates a unique device context for each window in the class. ParentDC = 128 Sets the clipping rectangle of the child window to that of the parent window so that the child can draw on the parent. A window from a class containing the ParentDC style receives a regular device context from the system's cache of device contexts. It does not give the child the parent's device context or device context settings. Specifying ParentDC enhances an application's performance. SaveBits = 2048 Saves, as a bitmap, the portion of the screen image obscured by a window of this class. When the window is removed, the system uses the saved bitmap to restore the screen image, including other windows that were obscured. Therefore, the system does not send WM_PAINT messages to windows that were obscured if the memory used by the bitmap has not been discarded and if other screen actions have not invalidated the stored image. This style is useful for small windows (for example, menus or dialog boxes) that are displayed briefly and then removed before other screen activity takes place. This style increases the time required to display the window, because the system must first allocate memory to store the bitmap. VRedraw = 1 Redraws the entire window if a movement or size adjustment changes the height of the client area.

api/FireBlade.WinInteropUtils.ComponentO.title: Enum COM.COMInitOptions | WinInteropUtils
api/FireBlade.WinInteropUtils.ComponentO.summary: Enum COM.COMInitOptions Namespace FireBlade.WinInteropUtils.ComponentObjectModel Assembly WinInteropUtils.dll Specifies flags for Initialize(COMInitOptions). [Flags] public enum COM.COMInitOptions Extension Methods EnumExtensions.GetValues(Enum) Fields ApartmentThreaded = 2 Initializes the thread for STA (Single-Threaded Apartment). DisableOle1DDE = 4 Disables DDE for OLE1 support. MultiThreaded = 0 Initializes the thread for MTA (Multi-Threaded Apartment). This is the default. SpeedOverMemory = 8 Increase memory usage in an attempt to increase performance. Remarks When a thread is initialized through a call to Initialize(COMInitOptions), you choose whether to initialize it as apartment-threaded or multithreaded by designating one of the members of COM.COMInitOptions as its parameter. This designates how incoming calls to any object created by that thread are handled, that is, the object's concurrency. Apartment-threading, while allowing for multiple threads of execution, serializes all incoming calls by requiring that calls to methods of objects created by this thread always run on the same thread, i.e. the apartment/thread that created them. In addition, calls can arrive only at message-queue boundaries. Because of this serialization, it is not typically necessary to write concurrency control into the code for the object, other than to avoid calls to PeekMessage and SendMessage(uint, nuint, nint) during processing that must not be interrupted by other method invocations or calls to other objects in the same apartment/thread. Multi-threading (also called free-threading) allows calls to methods of objects created by this thread to be run on any thread. There is no serialization of calls, i.e. many calls may occur to the same method or to the same object or simultaneously. Multi-threaded object concurrency offers the highest performance and takes the best advantage of multiprocessor hardware for cross-thread, cross-process, and cross-machine calling, since calls to objects are not serialized in any way.This means, however, that the code for objects must enforce its own concurrency model, typically through the use of synchronization primitives, such as critical sections, semaphores, or mutexes. In addition, because the object doesn't control the lifetime of the threads that are accessing it, no thread-specific state may be stored in the object (in Thread Local Storage). Note: The multi-threaded apartment is intended for use by non-GUI threads. Threads in multi-threaded apartments should not perform UI actions. This is because UI threads require a message pump, and COM does not pump messages for threads in a multi-threaded apartment.
